---
title: ""
page-layout: full
format:
  html:
    toc: false
---

```{=html}

<div class="site-page education-page">

<section class="site-intro">
  <div class="site-kicker">Teaching · Supervision · Mentoring</div>
  <h2>Education</h2>
  <div class="site-lede site-muted">
    <p>
    Education has always been close to my heart. As the eldest in my family, I grew up helping my two younger brothers with various subjects, through which I gained valuable perspectives on education. Beyond the familiar, funny sibling dynamic (often involving fiery arguments over seemingly nothing, clashes of opinions, and, sometimes, dramatic retelling of events to our parents), those moments taught me that learning is as much about connections and relationships as it is about knowledge.
    </p>
    <p>
    Supporting my first (and most adorable) students shaped my educational philosophy from an early age. It convinced me that the most meaningful learning emerges when curiosity leads the way, students take a proactive role in their own learning, and the process remains enjoyable. Since then, I have sought to cultivate learning environments that are curiosity-driven, student-led, and centred on the joy of discovery.
    </p>
<!--
		<p>
			<span class="site-lede">
		###
			</span>
		</p>
-->
  </div>
</section>

<section class="site-section education-philosophy">
  <h2 class="site-section-title">Educational approach</h2>
  <p class="education-section-lede">
  	My teaching draws on cybernetics, complexity, and hands-on learning to help students engage critically and creatively with the systems that shape our world.
    Across undergraduate, postgraduate, and non-award courses, I aim to create learning environments where students can move between conceptual understanding, practical experimentation, and reflective engagement with real-world complexity.
  </p>

  <div class="education-theme-grid">
    <article class="education-theme-card">
      <div class="education-card-kicker">Systems</div>
      <h3>Cybernetic thinking</h3>
      <p>Rather than focusing solely on individual parts, cybernetic thinking encourages us to understand the patterns of interaction in a system that give rise to collective behaviour and emergent outcomes, helping learners develop a richer understanding of interconnected systems.</p>
    </article>

    <article class="education-theme-card">
      <div class="education-card-kicker">Dialogue</div>
      <h3>Cross-disciplinarity</h3>
      <p>I am particularly interested in exploring universal patterns and ideas that transcend disciplinary boundaries, creating opportunities for conversations that connect different ways of knowing and broaden our understanding of the complex world we inhabit.</p>
    </article>
    
    <article class="education-theme-card">
      <div class="education-card-kicker">Practice</div>
      <h3>Learning by making</h3>
      <p>Students learn by building, testing, prototyping, and reflecting. I use hands-on activities to help learners encounter complexity not as an abstract idea, but as something they can observe, manipulate, and reason with.</p>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

<section class="site-band">
  <div class="site-section">
    <h2 class="site-section-title">Teaching engagements</h2>
    <p class="education-section-lede">
      My teaching engagement spans undergraduate, postgraduate, and non-award courses at ANU. The examples below highlight courses and learning experiences where I contribute to conceptual framing, facilitation, hands-on inquiry, and reflective learning design.
    </p>

    <div class="teaching-card-grid">
      <article class="teaching-card">
        <div class="teaching-card-meta">Master of Applied Cybernetics · ANU School of Cybernetics</div>
        <a class="title-link" href="https://programsandcourses.anu.edu.au/course/cybn8001">
        	<h3>CYBN8001: Building Cyber-Physical Systems I & II
</h3>
				</a>
        <p>As a core year-long course in the <a href="https://cybernetics.anu.edu.au/education/masters/">Master of Applied Cybernetics</a>, this course enables students to critically analyse emerging technological constellations and engage in informed conversations about cybernetic systems. Grounded in a learning-by-making ethos, the course supports students as they design and build cyber-physical system prototypes both individually and in diverse collaborative teams.</p>
        <div class="teaching-tags">
          <span>Postgraduate</span>
          <span>Cyber-physical systems</span>
          <span>Studio learning</span>
        </div>
      </article>

      <article class="teaching-card">
        <div class="teaching-card-meta">Learning Experiences · ANU School of Cybernetics</div>
        <a class="title-link" href="https://cybernetics.anu.edu.au/education/learning-experiences/">
        	<h3>Decoding/Leading AI through Cybernetics</h3>
        </a>
        <p>
        As part of the School's <i>Navigating Cybernetic Futures Learning Experience</i> (LX) series, I contribute to the delivery of this one-day non-award module. Working within a presenter team, I help equip participants from industry, government, and community sectors with clearer ways of understanding AI as part of wider social, cultural, economic, and ecological systems.
        </p>
        <div class="teaching-tags">
          <span>Non-award</span>
          <span>AI literacy</span>
          <span>Professional education</span>
        </div>
      </article>

      <article class="teaching-card">
        <div class="teaching-card-meta">Undergraduate Workshops · ANU McCusker Institute</div>
        <h3>Undergraduate teaching</h3>
        <!--Use this card for undergraduate courses, guest lectures, tutorials, workshops, or foundational teaching contributions. This can include mathematics, physics, data analysis, systems thinking, or computational methods, depending on what you want to foreground.-->
        <p>
        I developed and facilitate an undergraduate workshop within the <i>Cybernetics for Social Impact</i> curriculum (awarded <i>Transdisciplinary Teaching Award 2026</i>). Hosted by the <a href="https://mccuskerinstitute.anu.edu.au/">ANU McCusker Institute</a>, the workshop equips students with transdisciplinary tools and systems perspectives for engaging with complex social issues and creating meaningful impact.
        </p>
        <!--Through repeated delivery across successive cohorts, the workshop has become an evolving learning environment, continually refined through reflection, feedback, and experimentation.-->
        <!--The workshop is designed to provide students with space and time to think through what it means by self-organisation, interact with other peers to embody the concept, and understand what makes systems resilient. We discuss the emergence of patterns in natural and sociotechnical contexts and identify cybernetic elements, such as agents, relationships, and feedback, to analyse self-organising systems with the aim of envisioning resilient and hopeful futures.-->
        
        <div class="teaching-tags">
          <span>Undergraduate</span>
          <span>Transdisciplinarity</span>
          <span>Hopeful futures</span>
        </div>
      </article>
    </div>
  </div>
</section>


<!--
<section class="site-section education-practice-areas">
  <h2 class="site-section-title">Areas of educational practice</h2>
  <p class="education-section-lede">
    My teaching interests are closely connected to my research: I use complexity, cybernetics, topology, and human–AI relations as ways to help learners ask better questions about systems, technology, knowledge, and responsibility.
  </p>

  <div class="education-practice-list">
    <article class="education-practice-item">
      <h3>Complexity and cybernetics</h3>
      <p>Teaching systems as dynamic, feedback-driven, and situated helps learners work with uncertainty rather than reduce it prematurely.</p>
    </article>

    <article class="education-practice-item">
      <h3>Human–AI futures</h3>
      <p>I support learners in developing richer vocabularies for understanding generative AI beyond narrow technical framings, with attention to agency, accountability, context, and consequence.</p>
    </article>

    <article class="education-practice-item">
      <h3>Data, structure, and topology</h3>
      <p>I am interested in making mathematical and computational ways of seeing more accessible, especially where they help reveal hidden patterns, relations, and flows in complex systems.</p>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>
-->



<section class="site-section">
  <h2 class="site-section-title">Research supervision and mentoring</h2>
  <p class="education-section-lede">
    My supervision and mentoring practice is grounded in curiosity, care, and methodological openness. I am interested in supporting students whose projects engage with complexity, cybernetics, information dynamics, topology, AI-mediated systems, or related questions about emergence and knowledge systems.
  </p>

  <div class="supervision-grid">
    <article class="supervision-card">
      <h3>Research supervision</h3>
      <p>I welcome projects that combine conceptual, computational, empirical, or design-oriented approaches to understanding complex systems.</p>
    </article>

    <article class="supervision-card">
      <h3>Mentoring</h3>
      <p>Inspired by a <i>pay-it-forward</i> ethos, I aim to help students and emerging researchers clarify their questions, navigate uncertainty, and develop confidence in transdisciplinary work.</p>
    </article>

    <article class="supervision-card">
      <h3>Possible project directions</h3>
      <ul>
        <li>Modelling complex systems to deepen our understanding of pattern formation and the emergence of collective behaviour</li>
        <li>Topics related to human–AI interaction</li>
        <li>Information dynamics through language and communication</li>
        <!--<li>Structural diagnostics for knowledge integrity</li>-->
      </ul>
    </article>
  </div>
</section>

</div>
```
